content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/runner/work/my-harness/my-harness/.claude/skills/jira-cli

# Jira CLI

Interact with Atlassian Jira from the command line using [jira-cli](https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli).

## When to Use

- User asks to create, view, edit, or search Jira issues/tickets
- User needs to transition issues through workflow states (To Do → In Progress → Done)
- User wants to manage sprints, epics, or boards
- User needs to assign issues, add comments, or log work time
- User asks about their current tasks or sprint progress

## Prerequisites

1. Install jira-cli: `brew install ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/jira-cli` (macOS) or download from [releases](https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/releases)
2. Set API token: `export JIRA_API_TOKEN="your-token"`
3. Initialize: `jira init` and follow prompts

## Issue Commands

### List Issues

```bash
# List issues in current project
jira issue list

# List my assigned issues
jira issue list -a$(jira me)

# List issues by status
jira issue list -s"In Progress"

# List high priority issues
jira issue list -yHigh

# List issues with multiple filters
jira issue list -a$(jira me) -s"To Do" -yHigh --created week

# List issues with raw JQL
jira issue list -q "project = PROJ AND status = 'In Progress'"

# Plain text output for scripting
jira issue list --plain --columns key,summary,status --no-headers
```

### Create Issues

```bash
# Interactive issue creation
jira issue create

# Create with all options specified
jira issue create -tBug -s"Login button not working" -b"Description here" -yHigh --no-input

# Create a story
jira issue create -tStory -s"Add user authentication" -yMedium

# Create with labels and components
jira issue create -tTask -s"Update dependencies" -lmaintenance -l"tech-debt" -Cbackend

# Create and assign to self
jira issue create -tBug -s"Fix crash on startup" -a$(jira me) --no-input
```

### View Issues

```bash
# View issue details
jira issue view ISSUE-123

# View with comments
jira issue view ISSUE-123 --comments 10

# View in plain text
jira issue view ISSUE-123 --plain

# Open issue in browser
jira open ISSUE-123
```

### Edit Issues

```bash
# Edit summary
jira issue edit ISSUE-123 -s"Updated summary"

# Edit description
jira issue edit ISSUE-123 -b"New description"

# Edit priority
jira issue edit ISSUE-123 -yHigh

# Add labels
jira issue edit ISSUE-123 -lnew-label
```

### Transition Issues

```bash
# Move issue to a new status
jira issue move ISSUE-123 "In Progress"

# Move with comment
jira issue move ISSUE-123 "Done" --comment "Completed the task"

# Move and set resolution
jira issue move ISSUE-123 "Done" -RFixed
```

### Assign Issues

```bash
# Assign to self
jira issue assign ISSUE-123 $(jira me)

# Assign to specific user
jira issue assign ISSUE-123 username

# Unassign
jira issue assign ISSUE-123 x
```

### Comments

```bash
# Add a comment
jira issue comment add ISSUE-123 "This is my comment"

# Add comment from editor
jira issue comment add ISSUE-123
```

### Work Logging

```bash
# Log time
jira issue worklog add ISSUE-123 "2h 30m"

# Log time with comment
jira issue worklog add ISSUE-123 "1d 4h" --comment "Completed feature implementation" --no-input
```

### Link & Clone Issues

```bash
# Link two issues
jira issue link ISSUE-123 ISSUE-456 Blocks

# Unlink issues
jira issue unlink ISSUE-123 ISSUE-456

# Clone an issue
jira issue clone ISSUE-123 -s"Cloned: New summary"

# Delete an issue
jira issue delete ISSUE-123
```

## Epic Commands

```bash
# List epics
jira epic list

# List epics in table format
jira epic list --table

# Create an epic
jira epic create -n"Q1 Features" -s"Epic summary" -b"Epic description"

# Add issues to epic
jira epic add EPIC-1 ISSUE-123 ISSUE-456

# Remove issues from epic
jira epic remove ISSUE-123 ISSUE-456
```

## Sprint Commands

```bash
# List sprints
jira sprint list

# List current/active sprint
jira sprint list --current

# List my issues in current sprint
jira sprint list --current -a$(jira me)

# Add issues to sprint
jira sprint add SPRINT_ID ISSUE-123 ISSUE-456
```

## Project & Board Commands

```bash
# List projects
jira project list

# List boards
jira board list

# List releases/versions
jira release list

# Open project in browser
jira open
```

## Utility Commands

```bash
# Get current username
jira me

# Show help
jira --help
jira issue --help

# Setup shell completion
jira completion bash  # or zsh, fish, powershell
```

## Common Flags

| Flag | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `--plain` | Plain text output (no interactive UI) |
| `--raw` | Raw JSON output |
| `--csv` | CSV output |
| `--no-input` | Skip interactive prompts |
| `-t, --type` | Issue type (Bug, Story, Task, Epic) |
| `-s, --summary` | Issue summary/title |
| `-b, --body` | Issue description |
| `-y, --priority` | Priority (Highest, High, Medium, Low, Lowest) |
| `-l, --label` | Labels (repeatable) |
| `-a, --assignee` | Assignee username |
| `-r, --reporter` | Reporter username |
| `-C, --component` | Component name |
| `-P, --parent` | Parent issue/epic key |
| `-q, --jql` | Raw JQL query |
| `--created` | Filter by creation date (-7d, week, month) |
| `--order-by` | Sort field |
| `--reverse` | Reverse sort order |

## Common Workflows

### Start Working on an Issue

```bash
# Assign to self and move to In Progress
jira issue assign ISSUE-123 $(jira me)
jira issue move ISSUE-123 "In Progress"
```

### Complete an Issue

```bash
# Log work and close
jira issue worklog add ISSUE-123 "4h" --no-input
jira issue move ISSUE-123 "Done" --comment "Completed" -RFixed
```

### Daily Standup Review

```bash
# View my current sprint tasks
jira sprint list --current -a$(jira me)
```

### Create and Track a Bug

```bash
# Create bug
jira issue create -tBug -s"App crashes on login" -yHigh -lbug --no-input
# Note the returned issue key, then assign
jira issue assign BUG-123 $(jira me)
jira issue move BUG-123 "In Progress"
```

## Output Examples

| Command | Use Case |
| ------- | -------- |
| `jira issue list --plain` | Script-friendly output |
| `jira issue list --raw` | JSON for parsing |
| `jira issue list --csv` | Export to spreadsheet |

## Limitations

- Requires prior `jira init` configuration
- Some features may vary between Jira Cloud and Server
- Complex custom fields may require `--custom` flag with field IDs
- Rate limits apply based on Jira instance configuration

content.thinking:
Now I have enough information to assess each ticket. Let me check the existing agents more carefully:

Repo has:
- `agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md`, `agents/jira-ticket/README.md`
- `agents/pr-review/prompt.md`, `agents/pr-review/README.md`
- `agents/agent-improver/prompt.md`, `agents/agent-improver/README.md`
- `.github/workflows/pr-review.yml`
- `.github/workflows/jira-ticket.yml`
- `.github/workflows/agent-improver.yml`

But it's missing the pr-refinement, senior-dev, junior-dev, and testing agents along with their corresponding workflows. Now I can evaluate which tickets are ready to work on.

Starting with KAN-2, the junior developer agent is in the Idea stage and blocked by KAN-1 as expected. The ticket has a solid description with acceptance criteria, though it leaves three implementation decisions open: whether to use `/goal` or `/ralph-loop` framing (neither currently exists in the skills directory), which stop condition to apply, and what branch naming convention to follow. It references existing files like the jira-ticket agent's prompt and workflow, and it's designed to create three new files for the junior-dev agent.

The three files are tightly coupled as part of the same agent, making this one atomic concern for a single PR. Looking at the implementation decisions more carefully, the description frames these as choices the implementer should make and document in the README rather than questions requiring human input — the prompt framing approach needs to be defined, the stop condition selected from the three options, and the branch naming convention established. I'm realizing the distinction here: the ticket is delegating the choice to the implementer rather than asking for input from the ticket author. The "pick one each" framing means the engineer should make the decision themselves and document it, not that the ticket is waiting for someone to decide. But that still creates a problem for the readiness bar—if two engineers would genuinely pick different options, the ticket isn't truly unambiguous by that standard. I should probably flag these as needing human input to resolve the open choices, or examine them more closely to see if there's actually a clear preference implied. The branch naming convention in KAN-2 is actually a documented dependency for KAN-3, which explicitly needs to filter pull requests using whatever pattern KAN-2 establishes. So while the implementer has flexibility in choosing the pattern, it's a legitimate engineering decision the ticket is delegating to them. Looking at the dependency chain, KAN-2 needs to be completed before KAN-3 can proceed, KAN-4 depends on KAN-1 which is already done, and KAN-5 is...

KAN-5 stands independently. Each ticket creates three tightly coupled files—a prompt, README, and workflow configuration—that form a single atomic unit for one agent. These files can't be shipped separately since the workflow depends on the prompt, so they belong together in one ticket rather than being split into sub-tasks.

Now I need to verify whether these are ready to go or if they need the "Needs human input" label. Looking at the readiness criteria: the rubrics are complete with concrete titles, descriptions, context, and acceptance criteria. They're atomic as single PRs. For code-grounding, KAN-2 references existing paths in the repo, but KAN-3 references `agents/testing/` which doesn't exist yet—that's the target being created. KAN-3 also depends on branch naming conventions from KAN-2, which creates a dependency I need to trace.

KAN-4 references `docs/agentic-workflow.md` and existing workflows, but `/openspec:explore` isn't in `.claude/skills/` yet. KAN-5 references the target `agents/pr-refinement/` directory along with existing files.

On the ambiguity front, KAN-2 has open choices around the stop condition and prompt framing that two engineers could interpret differently. KAN-3's implementation depends on KAN-2's branch naming decision, which hasn't been finalized. KAN-4 leaves the spec exploration approach open-ended.

Actually, I need to reconsider what counts as an open question. The tickets frame these as "implementation decisions to document in README" — but the real question is whether these are decisions a typical engineer would confidently make on their own, or if they need product and architectural guidance from whoever wrote the ticket. Looking at KAN-2 specifically, the prompt framing choice between `/goal`, `/ralph-loop`, or direct approaches involves skill selections where neither `/goal` nor `/ralph-loop` currently exist.

KAN-3 has a hard dependency on KAN-2's branch naming decision before the workflow filter can be implemented. KAN-4 presents two significant architectural choices: whether to build a dedicated `/openspec:explore` skill, and how to handle the human-in-the-loop pause — either through a draft PR with a magic comment or a Jira comment with status transition. KAN-5 still needs to specify which Claude model to use instead of the default.

Looking at this through the readiness lens, two engineers would definitely implement these differently given the open choices. The tickets intentionally leave these as implementation decisions to document in the README, but that creates ambiguity at the grooming stage. The rubric says to default to "not ready" when in doubt, so I should probably flag the architectural decisions — particularly the human-in-the-loop mechanism in KAN-4 — as needing clarification before the tickets can be considered truly ready. The branch naming decision from KAN-2 is a dependency for KAN-3, but since KAN-3 is blocked until KAN-2 completes anyway, the naming will be established by the time it's needed. Looking at readiness across the tickets, KAN-5 has clear acceptance criteria with its implementation choices—model selection and stop conditions—being purely engineering decisions that don't affect external behavior. The AC actually pins this to the label-based approach by specifying "round cap," which implies storing a counter rather than detecting when a human posts. So even though the implementation details are framed as engineer choices, the acceptance criteria constrains the solution to option A.

Now I need to verify whether the `docs/agentic-workflow.md` file that the ticket references actually exists in the repo, since my initial file search only covered the `agents/` and `.github/workflows/` directories.

1	You are the **PR Review agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
2	checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
3	and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.
4	
5	## Your job
6	
7	Review the changes in this PR and post review feedback as GitHub comments.
8	Nothing else.
9	
10	## What to look for
11	
12	Focus on these four buckets, in this order:
13	
14	1. **Correctness bugs** — logic errors, off-by-ones, wrong conditions,
15	   misuse of APIs, broken error handling, race conditions.
16	2. **Security issues** — injection risks, auth bypass, leaked secrets,
17	   unsafe deserialization, SSRF, missing input validation at trust
18	   boundaries.
19	3. **Performance** — only when the cost is clearly meaningful (N+1 queries,
20	   accidental quadratic loops, unbounded memory). Don't nitpick micro-perf.
21	4. **Code quality** — only when it materially hurts readability or
22	   maintainability. Don't enforce style preferences.
23	
24	Skim out of scope: formatting, naming bikeshedding, "have you considered…"
25	musings, hypothetical future requirements.
26	
27	## How to gather context
28	
29	- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
30	- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. This is the primary input.

file.content:
You are the **PR Review agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.

## Your job

Review the changes in this PR and post review feedback as GitHub comments.
Nothing else.

## What to look for

Focus on these four buckets, in this order:

1. **Correctness bugs** — logic errors, off-by-ones, wrong conditions,
   misuse of APIs, broken error handling, race conditions.
2. **Security issues** — injection risks, auth bypass, leaked secrets,
   unsafe deserialization, SSRF, missing input validation at trust
   boundaries.
3. **Performance** — only when the cost is clearly meaningful (N+1 queries,
   accidental quadratic loops, unbounded memory). Don't nitpick micro-perf.
4. **Code quality** — only when it materially hurts readability or
   maintainability. Don't enforce style preferences.

Skim out of scope: formatting, naming bikeshedding, "have you considered…"
musings, hypothetical future requirements.

## How to gather context

- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. This is the primary input.

file.content:
name: PR Review agent

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, ready_for_review, reopened]
    paths-ignore:
      - '.github/agent-logs/**'
      - 'agents/**/prompt.md'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
      id-token: write
      actions: write
      checks: read
      statuses: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1

      - id: prompt
        run: |
          {
            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
            echo "REPO: ${{ github.repository }}"

content.thinking:
Good. `docs/agentic-workflow.md` exists. Now let me do my full assessment of the 4 tickets.

### Summary of Tickets

**KAN-2**: Build Junior Developer agent
- Has good description, context, AC
- **Blocked by KAN-1** (DEVELOPMENT READY) ✓ - blocker link already exists
- Implementation decisions: prompt framing, stop condition, branch naming
- These are all engineering choices left to the implementer
- Code-grounded: references `agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md` ✓, `.github/workflows/jira-ticket.yml` ✓, `docs/agentic-workflow.md` ✓
- The AC is concrete and testable, and the implementation parameters are documented as design choices for the engineer to resolve
- No sub-tasks needed since the three tightly coupled files form a single agent deliverable in one PR
- The description provides enough clarity that the engineer can make informed implementation decisions while the AC validates the observable behavior regardless of which option they choose

The stop condition testing is straightforward — it verifies that some condition exists and is documented, not which specific one. For branch naming, KAN-2 will establish the convention before KAN-3 starts, so there's no ambiguity. Looking at the rubric, KAN-2 has a clear title, concrete description of what to do, relevant context, and testable acceptance criteria. I think this is actually ready to go.

On atomicity, the three files — the prompt, README, and workflow — form a single cohesive unit that can't be meaningfully merged separately. The "implementation decisions" sections are engineering choices to make during work, not unresolved product questions. After a final check, all four tickets appear to meet the rubric standards.

KAN-2 passes cleanly with all required fields, atomic scope, code-grounded references, and the correct blocker relationship to KAN-1. KAN-3 depends on KAN-2's branch naming pattern decision, which is acceptable given the explicit blocking relationship. But looking at KAN-3's structure more carefully, it has Goal, Deliverables, Loop-back mechanism, and Acceptance criteria — yet the rubric requires Description, Context, and AC sections specifically. The "exactly once" phrasing in the acceptance criteria is describing the test scenario rather than mandating a global hard cap of 1. The real requirement is just that a hard cap exists to prevent infinite loops between the Junior and Testing agents, though the specific cap value isn't defined. KAN-3 does have the proper blocking dependency on KAN-2 for the branch naming convention.

For KAN-3 itself, the context is self-evident from the goal description—you need tests to catch bugs before merge—so skipping an explicit Context section is justified. The ticket has all other rubric elements and the loop-back mechanism is a valid engineering choice, so it's ready to proceed.

Now looking at KAN-4, which is the Senior Developer agent. It has the description, context, and acceptance criteria, with a proper blocking dependency on KAN-1 which is already development ready. I'm starting to map out the implementation decisions, beginning with the spec exploration approach.

The first decision is around the `/openspec:explore` command—it's not a built-in action, so I need to either create it or find an alternative. The description already flags this as something the implementer should verify against what's actually available in the Claude Code Action. The second decision is more architectural: how to handle the human-in-the-loop pause. I could either write the spec to a draft PR and wait for a magic-keyword comment, or post it as a Jira comment and resume on a ticket transition or label change. These are functionally different in terms of where the human interacts and approves, but the acceptance criteria just requires that some defined human approval action exists to resume the workflow into implementation.

The implementer gets to choose which mechanism works best for their setup, document it in the README, and the AC tests the observable behavior. For KAN-4, all the rubric elements are there—it's atomic, code-grounded with references to the workflow file and skills directory, and doesn't need a "Needs human input" section. It's ready to go. Now I'm moving on to KAN-5, which is about building a PR Refinement agent.

For KAN-5, I have the description, context, and acceptance criteria. The pr-review agent already exists so there's no blocking dependency. On the implementation side, I need to pick a different model than pr-review and verify it against Anthropic's docs. For the stop condition, the AC specifically mentions "round cap," which in context means a label-based counter approach—storing how many refinement rounds have happened. The alternative of freezing when a human posts is a different concept entirely, not really a "round cap" in the traditional sense. The AC is implicitly pointing toward the counter-based approach.

Looking at the KAN-5 rubric: all elements are present, it's atomic, code-grounded with the prompt file and workflow configuration, and there are no open questions. This ticket is ready to go.

All four tickets appear to be in good shape. Let me verify there aren't any hidden sub-tasks or dependencies I'm overlooking. The key tension here is whether "no hidden decisions" means the approach must be fully settled before implementation starts, or whether it's acceptable to delegate architectural choices to the engineer with clear guidance. These tickets explicitly ask the implementer to pick between options and document the choice, which technically leaves decisions unsettled at ticket creation time. I'm reconsidering whether I should add "Needs human input" sections to clarify which approach to use upfront, or if the current framing—where the engineer decides during implementation—still counts as ready-to-work tickets. These are parent Tasks, not sub-tasks, so the atomicity criteria apply differently. Looking at the "no open questions" criterion—none of these tickets have unchecked items in a needs-human-input section, so that's satisfied. For unambiguity, the observable behavior each acceptance criterion tests is clear enough that two engineers would build the same thing: KAN-2 produces a workflow that creates a branch and PR with logging and naming rules, KAN-3 creates a PR-triggered workflow that runs tests with a hard cap, KAN-4 generates a spec artifact and pauses for approval, and KAN-5 sets up a daily cron that pushes back on PRs.

Implementation details like which model or stop mechanism to use would vary, but since the acceptance criteria define observable behavior, the tickets are unambiguous from that perspective. All four have complete rubrics, are atomic, reference existing code paths, and have no open questions. The blocker dependencies are already linked correctly—KAN-2 blocks KAN-3, KAN-1 blocks both KAN-2 and KAN-4, and KAN-5 stands alone since it just references the other workflows. will be determined once KAN-2 is complete, so the acceptance criteria doesn't need to specify it upfront. I'm also noticing that KAN-3 uses a different heading structure than the rubric suggests, but the content is all there and clear enough — I shouldn't edit it just for consistency. None of these tickets need my intervention since they already meet the bar.

Let me finalize my assessment: KAN-2, KAN-3, KAN-4, and KAN-5 are all ready to go. Time to write up the Slack summary.

Actually, I should double-check KAN-5 one more time to make sure I'm not missing anything.

file.content:
name: PR Review agent

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, ready_for_review, reopened]
    paths-ignore:
      - '.github/agent-logs/**'
      - 'agents/**/prompt.md'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
      id-token: write
      actions: write
      checks: read
      statuses: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1

      - id: prompt
        run: |
          {
            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
            echo "REPO: ${{ github.repository }}"
            echo "PR NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
            echo
            cat agents/pr-review/prompt.md
            echo PROMPT_EOF
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - id: claude
        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
          track_progress: false
          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
          settings: |
            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
          claude_args: |
            --allowedTools "mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch,Bash(gh pr comment:*),Bash(gh pr diff:*),Bash(gh pr view:*),Read,Grep,Glob"

      - name: Commit execution log to PR branch
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
          BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
        run: |
          set -e
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git fetch --depth=1 origin "$BRANCH"
          git checkout -B "$BRANCH" "origin/$BRANCH"
          DEST=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json"
          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
          git add "$DEST"
          git commit -m "agent log: PR #${PR_NUMBER} run ${RUN_ID}"
          git push origin "$BRANCH"

      - name: Trigger agent improver
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_REF: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
        run: |
          gh workflow run agent-improver.yml \
            -f agent_name=pr-review \
            -f log_path=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json" \
            -f log_ref="${LOG_REF}"

file.content:
name: Jira Ticket agent

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '17 7 * * *'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  groom:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      id-token: write
      actions: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1

      - name: Install jira CLI
        run: bash scripts/install-jira-cli.sh

      - name: Put bin/ on PATH
        run: echo "$PWD/bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"

      - name: Write jira config
        env:
          JIRA_SERVER: ${{ vars.JIRA_SERVER }}
          JIRA_LOGIN: ${{ vars.JIRA_LOGIN }}
          JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT_KEY }}
          JIRA_BOARD_ID: ${{ vars.JIRA_BOARD_ID }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          : "${JIRA_SERVER:?JIRA_SERVER repo variable is required}"
          : "${JIRA_LOGIN:?JIRA_LOGIN repo variable is required}"
          : "${JIRA_PROJECT_KEY:?JIRA_PROJECT_KEY repo variable is required}"
          mkdir -p .jira
          cat > .jira/config.yml <<EOF
          installation: Cloud
          server: ${JIRA_SERVER}
          login: ${JIRA_LOGIN}
          project:
              key: ${JIRA_PROJECT_KEY}
              type: classic
          board:
              id: ${JIRA_BOARD_ID:-0}
          EOF

      - id: prompt
        env:
          JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT_KEY }}
        run: |
          {
            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
            echo "JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${JIRA_PROJECT_KEY}"
            echo
            cat agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md
            echo PROMPT_EOF
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - id: claude
        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        env:
          JIRA_CONFIG_FILE: ${{ github.workspace }}/.jira/config.yml
          JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}
          JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT_KEY }}
        with:
          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
          settings: |
            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
          claude_env: |
            JIRA_CONFIG_FILE: ${{ github.workspace }}/.jira/config.yml
            JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}
            JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT_KEY }}
          claude_args: |
            --allowedTools "Bash(jira:*),Bash(bin/jira:*),Read,Glob,Grep,Write,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch"

      - name: Post Slack summary
        if: always() && hashFiles('slack-summary.md') != ''
        env:
          SLACK_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.SLACK_MCP_XOXB_TOKEN }}
          CHANNEL_ID: ${{ vars.SLACK_OBSERVABILITY_CHANNEL_ID }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          : "${SLACK_TOKEN:?SLACK_MCP_XOXB_TOKEN secret is required}"
          : "${CHANNEL_ID:?SLACK_OBSERVABILITY_CHANNEL_ID repo variable is required}"
          payload=$(jq -n \
            --arg channel "${CHANNEL_ID}" \
            --rawfile text slack-summary.md \
            '{channel: $channel, text: $text, mrkdwn: true}')
          resp=$(curl -sS -X POST https://slack.com/api/chat.postMessage \
            -H "Authorization: Bearer ${SLACK_TOKEN}" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8" \
            --data "${payload}")
          echo "${resp}" | jq .
          [ "$(echo "${resp}" | jq -r .ok)" = "true" ]

      - name: Commit execution log to main
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
        run: |
          set -e
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git fetch --depth=1 origin main
          git checkout -B main origin/main
          DATE="$(date -u +%F)"
          DEST=".github/agent-logs/jira-ticket/${DATE}/${RUN_ID}.json"
          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
          git add "$DEST"
          git commit -m "agent log: jira-ticket run ${RUN_ID}"
          git push origin main

      - name: Trigger agent improver
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:

content.thinking:
Now I have a complete picture. Let me finalize my assessment:

**pr-review.yml**: Does NOT specify a `--model` in `claude_args`. This means it uses the default model for `claude-code-action@v1`. For KAN-5, the refinement agent should use a different model. Since the pr-review doesn't specify a model (uses default), the implementer needs to look up current model IDs and pick a different one. This is actionable.

Now let me make my final assessment of all 4 tickets:

**KAN-2**: Build Junior Developer agent
- Description is concrete with clear files to create
- Context explains why it matters
- Has 4 testable acceptance criteria
- Scoped to one agent and one PR
- References specific files like the jira-ticket prompt, workflow, and documentation
- Implementation decisions are left to the engineer while AC tests observable behavior
- No open questions blocking it
- Correctly depends on KAN-1

KAN-3 looks solid too—testing agent with 3 testable criteria, properly blocked by KAN-2, and the target directory structure is clear even though it doesn't exist yet. KAN-4 is starting to come into view with the Senior Developer agent, also checking the boxes for description, context, and acceptance criteria.

But I'm noticing something about KAN-4 that's giving me pause. The human-in-the-loop mechanism has two genuinely different architectural paths—GitHub-based versus Jira-based—and these aren't just implementation details. Two engineers would build fundamentally different systems depending on which one gets chosen, which means the ticket violates the unambiguous criterion. This needs human input before it's ready.

Though now I'm second-guessing myself on what counts as a "pick one" decision. Looking back at KAN-2, it also has a stop condition choice, but the acceptance criteria there is agnostic to which option gets picked—it just requires that whatever's chosen gets documented and tested. That's different from KAN-4, where the choice itself shapes the entire architecture. I think the engineer could reasonably pick one by looking at the existing agents and choosing the pattern that fits best, since the project already uses both GitHub and Jira. But I'm going to flag KAN-4's human-in-the-loop mechanism as needing input because it's a user-facing architectural decision where two engineers would build different systems, and I can't resolve it from the ticket alone. For KAN-2, KAN-3, and KAN-5, the open choices are pure engineering decisions that don't affect user-facing behavior, so those are fine to delegate.

Actually, let me reconsider whether KAN-4's spec exploration approach also needs flagging. The ticket acknowledges the ambiguity and says to verify what's available in the action first — that's an actionable engineering decision, not a product decision. The acceptance criteria is agnostic to implementation, so I don't need to flag this either.

For KAN-4, I'm only flagging the human-in-the-loop pause mechanism. Now I have my final readiness assessment: KAN-2, KAN-3, and KAN-5 are ready, but KAN-4 needs human input to decide which pause mechanism to use. I'll update KAN-4's description to add a "Needs human input" section, then write the Slack summary.

Since the description contains markdown headings, I need to write it to a temp file first and pipe it through the edit command to avoid injection issues.

The Senior Dev agent handles complex tickets by producing a spec and pausing for human approval before implementation. It depends on KAN-1 routing tickets that are too complex for the Junior Dev agent, and I need to decide between creating a custom skill for spec exploration or defining an equivalent approach directly in the prompt—I'll verify what's available in the Claude Code action first and document the choice. For the human-in-the-loop pause, I'm weighing options like using a draft PR to gate resumption. I need to make sure the `.jira/` directory exists before writing the temporary file there.

content.text: I have everything I need. Let me check the `.jira/` directory and then prepare the KAN-4 update.

content.thinking:
Good, `.jira/` exists. Now I'll write the updated KAN-4 description to a temp file and then run the edit command.

Let me analyze all tickets one final time:

1. **KAN-2** (Build Junior Developer agent): READY — rubric complete, atomic, code-grounded, unambiguous, no open questions, blocker linked
2. **KAN-3** (Build Testing agent): READY — same assessment 
3. **KAN-4** (Build Senior Developer agent): NOT READY — human-in-the-loop mechanism is an architectural user-facing choice between GitHub and Jira as control plane, cannot be resolved from ticket text
4. **KAN-5** (Build PR Refinement agent): READY — implementation decisions are engineering choices; the acceptance criteria implicitly specifies a round-counter stop condition

Now I'm updating the KAN-4 description with a section clarifying what needs human input.

content.text: Assessing all 4 tickets. KAN-2, KAN-3, KAN-5 are ready — their open "implementation decisions" are engineering choices, the ACs test observable behavior regardless of which option is picked. KAN-4 has one genuine ambiguity: the human-in-the-loop pause mechanism is an architectural choice (GitHub draft PR vs Jira comment) that changes where humans interact and can't be resolved from the ticket text. I'll add a `Needs human input` section to KAN-4 and then write the Slack summary.

input.content:
## Description

Create the Senior Developer agent: a workflow for complex tickets that produces a spec artifact and pauses for human approval before implementing.

Three files to create:
- `agents/senior-dev/prompt.md`
- `agents/senior-dev/README.md`
- `.github/workflows/senior-dev.yml` — modeled on `.github/workflows/jira-ticket.yml`; triggered via `workflow_dispatch` and/or `repository_dispatch` from the orchestrator

## Context

Handles the complex-ticket path in the agentic workflow (`docs/agentic-workflow.md`). The Development Orchestrator (KAN-1) routes here when a ticket is too complex for the Junior Dev agent. A human checkpoint between spec and implementation reduces wasted work on misunderstood requirements. Depends on KAN-1 being complete.

## Implementation decisions (pick one each; document the choice in README)

**Spec exploration approach:** `docs/agentic-workflow.md` references `/openspec:explore`. This is not a built-in Claude Code command and does not exist in `.claude/skills/`. The implementer must either create a custom skill at `.claude/skills/openspec/` or define an equivalent approach directly in the prompt. Verify what is actually available in `anthropics/claude-code-action@v1` before deciding. Document the choice and rationale in `agents/senior-dev/README.md`.

**Human-in-the-loop pause** (pick one):
- Write the spec to a draft PR and resume only when a human posts a magic-keyword comment (e.g. `!approve-spec`).
- Post the spec as a Jira comment and resume on a ticket transition or label.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Dispatching the workflow on a complex KAN ticket produces a spec artifact and pauses execution.
- A defined human approval action resumes the workflow into implementation.
- Run log committed under `.github/agent-logs/senior-dev/<DATE>/<RUN_ID>.json`.

## Needs human input

- [ ] Which human-in-the-loop mechanism should the spec approval use: (A) draft PR with magic-keyword comment (e.g. `!approve-spec`), or (B) Jira comment with ticket transition/label? This determines where humans review and approve the spec — pick the option that fits the team's review workflow.

content.content: File created successfully at: /home/runner/work/my-harness/my-harness/.jira/desc-tmp.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

Three files to create:
- `agents/senior-dev/prompt.md`
- `agents/senior-dev/README.md`
- `.github/workflows/senior-dev.yml` — modeled on `.github/workflows/jira-ticket.yml`; triggered via `workflow_dispatch` and/or `repository_dispatch` from the orchestrator

## Context

Handles the complex-ticket path in the agentic workflow (`docs/agentic-workflow.md`). The Development Orchestrator (KAN-1) routes here when a ticket is too complex for the Junior Dev agent. A human checkpoint between spec and implementation reduces wasted work on misunderstood requirements. Depends on KAN-1 being complete.

## Implementation decisions (pick one each; document the choice in README)

**Spec exploration approach:** `docs/agentic-workflow.md` references `/openspec:explore`. This is not a built-in Claude Code command and does not exist in `.claude/skills/`. The implementer must either create a custom skill at `.claude/skills/openspec/` or define an equivalent approach directly in the prompt. Verify what is actually available in `anthropics/claude-code-action@v1` before deciding. Document the choice and rationale in `agents/senior-dev/README.md`.

**Human-in-the-loop pause** (pick one):
- Write the spec to a draft PR and resume only when a human posts a magic-keyword comment (e.g. `!approve-spec`).
- Post the spec as a Jira comment and resume on a ticket transition or label.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Dispatching the workflow on a complex KAN ticket produces a spec artifact and pauses execution.
- A defined human approval action resumes the workflow into implementation.
- Run log committed under `.github/agent-logs/senior-dev/<DATE>/<RUN_ID>.json`.

## Needs human input

- [ ] Which human-in-the-loop mechanism should the spec approval use: (A) draft PR with magic-keyword comment (e.g. `!approve-spec`), or (B) Jira comment with ticket transition/label? This determines where humans review and approve the spec — pick the option that fits the team's review workflow.

content.text:
Done. Summary of the run:

**Ready (3 tickets)** — KAN-2, KAN-3, and KAN-5 all pass the readiness bar. Each ticket has a complete description, context, and concrete AC; creates one tightly-coupled agent (3 files) in a single PR; references paths that exist in the repo; and has the correct blocker links. The open "implementation decisions" in each are engineering-delegated choices (stop condition, model selection, branch naming convention) where the AC tests observable behavior regardless of which option is picked.

**Awaiting human input (1 ticket)** — KAN-4's human-in-the-loop pause mechanism is an architectural decision between two fundamentally different control planes (GitHub draft PR vs Jira comment/transition) that changes where humans review and approve specs. Two engineers would build different systems; the ticket text gives no preference. A `## Needs human input` checkbox has been added to KAN-4 asking the reporter to pick option A or B.
